CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; main contributions (CANE and its mutual-attention context-aware text embeddings) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables (reporting AUC and classification accuracy on Cora, HepTh, Zhihu against sensible baselines) are effective and informative. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the lack of any stated limitations or discussion of assumptions slightly weakens presentation completeness from a critical-theory perspective. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly: the intro, method, experiments and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions (context-aware embeddings and mutual attention) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (reporting AUC and accuracy on Cora, HepTh, Zhihu vs several baselines) effectively convey results. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the paper omits discussion of limitations and code release which would aid reproducibility and some readers may want clearer pointers to reproduce the mutual-attention implementation details. From a presentation standpoint this is a solid, practitioner-friendly paper with only minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; main contributions (CANE and its mutual-attention context-aware text embeddings) are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables (reporting AUC and classification accuracy on Cora, HepTh, Zhihu against sensible baselines) are effective and informative. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the lack of any stated limitations or discussion of assumptions slightly weakens presentation completeness from a critical-theory perspective.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly: the intro, method, experiments and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions (context-aware embeddings and mutual attention) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (reporting AUC and accuracy on Cora, HepTh, Zhihu vs several baselines) effectively convey results. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the paper omits discussion of limitations and code release which would aid reproducibility and some readers may want clearer pointers to reproduce the mutual-attention implementation details. From a presentation standpoint this is a solid, practitioner-friendly paper with only minor omissions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on multiple datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. It also concedes when prior methods are comparable (vertex classification) and claims improvements for link prediction, and related work coverage is described as adequate. However, from the summary it is unclear whether more recent or specialized text-/context-aware baselines were included or whether deeper analytic dissection of why CANE outperforms each baseline was provided; lack of code disclosure also weakens reproducibility. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in breadth and in-depth analysis. | B(4): The paper compares against a solid set of strong baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair comparison. It also transparently reports when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification) and highlights significant gains on link prediction. Coverage of related work is described as adequate and the methodological differences (mutual attention, context-aware text embeddings combined with structure embeddings) are clearly presented. Minor omissions remain—e.g., inclusion of additional recent text-aware/graph neural baselines or released code would strengthen reproducibility—so I do not rate it fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on multiple datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. It also concedes when prior methods are comparable (vertex classification) and claims improvements for link prediction, and related work coverage is described as adequate. However, from the summary it is unclear whether more recent or specialized text-/context-aware baselines were included or whether deeper analytic dissection of why CANE outperforms each baseline was provided; lack of code disclosure also weakens reproducibility. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in breadth and in-depth analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a solid set of strong baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair comparison. It also transparently reports when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification) and highlights significant gains on link prediction. Coverage of related work is described as adequate and the methodological differences (mutual attention, context-aware text embeddings combined with structure embeddings) are clearly presented. Minor omissions remain—e.g., inclusion of additional recent text-aware/graph neural baselines or released code would strengthen reproducibility—so I do not rate it fully comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses reasonable datasets, metrics, and strong baselines, and that related work coverage is adequate. They also concur that ablation/sensitivity analyses, discussion of limitations/failure cases, and public code are missing, which weakens reproducibility and depth of empirical insight. Given these strengths but the notable gaps in analysis and transparency, a middle score reflects solid experimental grounding but insufficient dissection to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is limited: although three datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and relevant baselines are used, there is no evidence of multi-seed stability or broad task diversity to robustly support the claimed improvements. Ablation work appears minimal — while the paper contrasts context-free and context-aware text embeddings, it lacks the comprehensive, controlled ablations (≥3 isolated component studies) and sensitivity analyses I expect. Related work coverage is adequate, but the authors do not discuss failure cases or limitations, and code availability is not mentioned despite only hyperparameters being reported, which hampers reproducibility. From a theory-first perspective the empirical gains are therefore insufficiently dissected to be convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are solid: three datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and appropriate evaluation metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) with comparisons to strong baselines including both structure-only (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec) and text-aware methods (TADW, CENE), which supports the main claims. The paper reports hyperparameters and uses sensible evaluation protocols, but the summary does not mention explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond the context-free vs. context-aware variants nor any discussion of failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is described as adequate. Reproducibility is partially supported (hyperparameters) but code release is not mentioned, lowering confidence; overall this is a thorough empirical paper with minor gaps in analysis and transparency.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is not a radical paradigm shift but does introduce a meaningful, non-trivial twist by using mutual attention to produce context-aware pairwise text embeddings combined with structural embeddings. Given the clear differentiation from prior text-aware network methods and demonstrated empirical gains, the contribution counts as a notable novelty rather than merely incremental engineering, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper adds a context-aware twist to network embedding by introducing a mutual attention mechanism to produce text-based vertex embeddings and concatenating these with structure-based embeddings. This is a moderate, targeted innovation rather than a radically new paradigm: the problem (network embedding/link prediction) is well-studied and many baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) already combine structure and text in various ways. While the mutual attention idea is a non-trivial architectural choice and the authors clearly differentiate it from context-free text embeddings, the overall technique reads as an incremental extension of existing methods (negative sampling, Adam optimization, and concatenated representations) that would not greatly surprise experts in the field.

reviewer_b.justification: CANE introduces a clear, practically useful twist—context-aware (pairwise) text embeddings via a mutual attention mechanism combined with structure embeddings—that is meaningfully different from prior text-aware/network methods like TADW and CENE. The paper frames a well-studied problem (network embedding) but contributes a non-trivial modelic extension (mutual attention to produce interaction-specific text representations) and empirically demonstrates gains on link prediction across multiple datasets. Given the baseline comparisons and explicit differentiation of its context-aware component, the idea is a notable novelty rather than merely an application of existing techniques.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree CANE is an important, practical empirical advance that yields consistent link-prediction gains and is likely to influence applied work on combining text and graph structure. The main reservations—lack of formal analysis, limited causal ablations, and missing code—reduce its theoretical novelty and broad reproducibility, but these do not outweigh the clear engineering and empirical contribution, so a slightly above-average impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: CANE tackles an important and timely problem—context-aware vertex representations and a mutual-attention mechanism that yields clear empirical gains on link prediction across datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) compared to established baselines. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is tempered: the summary reports no formal analysis, no stated limitations, and does not indicate comprehensive causal ablations or released code to enable broad adoption and scrutiny. The contribution is a useful empirical advance that will inform follow-up work, but without theoretical justification or extensive controlled studies it is unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers think about representation learning.

reviewer_b.justification: CANE addresses an important, practical problem—combining text and graph structure via context-aware vertex embeddings—and introduces a mutual-attention mechanism that is intuitively useful for relation modeling. The paper demonstrates significant link-prediction gains over strong baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on multiple datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and reports hyperparameters, which suggests its approach is likely to influence applied work on text+structure embeddings. While not necessarily transformative, CANE provides a clear, reproducible engineering advance that should open useful follow-on work and be adopted by researchers and practitioners working on graph+text tasks.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear claim and provides empirical evidence across multiple datasets that supports its conclusions, but lacks formal theoretical guarantees, extensive ablation rigor, and statistical testing for robustness. Given that the mathematical formulation appears correct and experimental results are convincing though not perfectly controlled or fully reproducible, a slightly positive assessment is warranted: the work is sound in practice but has methodological gaps that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claim (context-aware embeddings via mutual attention improve link prediction) is clearly stated and supported by empirical gains on Cora, HepTh and Zhihu, but no formal proofs or theoretical guarantees are provided. There appear to be no mathematical derivations or correctness proofs for the mutual-attention mechanism, and design choices (loss terms, negative sampling) lack analytical justification. Experimentally, standard baselines are used, but I see no mention of multi-seed stability, statistical tests, or the required ≥3 controlled ablations to isolate architectural contributions. Overall the results suggest the method works in practice, but missing theoretical motivation, limited ablation rigor, and incomplete experimental controls raise substantive technical concerns.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims (context-aware text embeddings via mutual attention improve relation modeling) and supports them with experiments showing significant link prediction gains and comparable vertex classification across three datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu). The mathematical formulation appears straightforward and there are no indicated derivation errors in the summary, though no formal proofs were required. Experimental methodology is broadly sound—comparisons include both structure-only and text-aware baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) and hyperparameters are reported—but I note the absence of statistical significance testing, limited dataset diversity, and no mention of released code, which reduces reproducibility confidence. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the main claims.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper fits squarely within graph/text representation learning, uses standard datasets and baselines, and provides relevant empirical results for the venue. The lower score reflects Reviewer A’s concern about limited theoretical motivation, missing ablations and code, and somewhat narrow empirical analysis; however, Reviewer B’s point about solid practical relevance and appropriate evaluation supports acceptance. Given this balance, a score of 4 reflects good fit with room for stronger methodological analysis and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (network embeddings / relation modeling) and the proposed CANE architecture (structure + text embeddings with mutual attention) fall squarely within top ML/NLP venues and evaluations (AUC, classification on Cora, HepTh, Zhihu against standard baselines) target the right audience. However, from the summary the paper lacks theoretical motivation or formal guarantees, does not report limitations or code, and appears to rely on empirical gains on a small suite of datasets without comprehensive causal ablations of each architectural choice (e.g., attention vs. baselines, loss components, negative sampling effects). Given these methodological gaps relative to the community’s expectations for provable or thoroughly dissected contributions, the fit is borderline rather than a clear accept.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the intersection of graph representation learning and text-aware modeling and thus is well within scope for a top ML/NLP venue: it proposes a practical model (CANE) combining structure and context-aware text embeddings with a mutual-attention mechanism and optimizes with standard negative sampling/Adam. The evaluation uses standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu), appropriate baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) and common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) with reported hyperparameters, matching the community’s empirical expectations. From a practical-empirical perspective the focus on clear empirical gains for link prediction and sensible comparison set makes it highly relevant to the venue’s audience.

main_claims: Proposes CANE, a network embedding model that learns context-aware embeddings for vertices depending on the interaction context.
Introduces a mutual attention mechanism to produce context-aware text-based embeddings and combines them with structure-based embeddings.
Shows CANE achieves significant improvement over state-of-the-art methods on link prediction and comparable performance on vertex classification.
_summary.methodology: CANE represents each vertex by concatenating a structure-based embedding and a text-based embedding (which can be context-free or context-aware via mutual attention); it optimizes edge objectives (structure- and text-based) using negative sampling and Adam.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a research focus on theoretical foundations of representation learning and provable guarantees for graph methods.
traits: Penalizes papers that lack formal/theoretical analysis or clear statements of assumptions (e.g., no propositions about representational capacity or learning guarantees).
Requires comprehensive ablation studies that isolate each architectural contribution; will downgrade if fewer than 3 controlled ablations are provided.
Discounts empirical claims that rely on improvements in a small number of metrics/datasets or single-seed experiments; expects multi-dataset, multi-seed stability evidence.
Deducts points when model design choices (e.g., loss terms, negative sampling, hyperparameters) are not justified analytically or via intuition linking to performance.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes theoretical justification and careful causal ablation; typically gives low scores to empirically-driven papers that lack formal motivation or rigorous dissection of components.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricalist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist working on applied graph representation learning and NLP-augmented network models, with many deployed systems that exploit text+structure embeddings.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements on standard tasks and benchmarks, especially consistent gains in link prediction or information retrieval metrics across datasets.
Favors novel model mechanisms that combine text and graph structure (e.g., attention-based context-aware embeddings) and will upweight papers introducing intuitive, practical architectures.
Requires comparisons to strong, relevant baselines (at least 3 including both structure-only and text-aware methods) but is tolerant of limited formal analysis if experiments are thorough.
Bonuses for reproducibility: explicit training details, hyperparameter ranges, and ideally released code; absence lowers confidence but is not an automatic rejection.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Values practical novelty and strong empirical evidence; typically gives higher scores to well-engineered, reproducible methods that show clear gains on real tasks even without deep theoretical proofs.
